# cq -- semantic code query tool
# 71 languages. One binary. Zero setup.
# tree-sitter parsing, stack graph resolution, optional LSP precision.
# Languages auto-install on first use via WASM plugins. Works on broken code.

## Commands (all accept --json, --raw, --project, --in, --lang, --kind)
def <symbol>          Find where a symbol is defined
body <symbol>         Full source body of a symbol
sig <symbol>          Type signature only
refs <symbol>         All references (call sites, imports, type uses)
callers <symbol>      Call sites only (subset of refs)
deps <symbol>         What a function calls/references in its body
outline <file>        All symbols in a file
symbols [--kind K]    All symbols in project
imports <file>        Imports/dependencies of a file
context <file>:<line> Enclosing symbol for a line number
tree [path]           Hierarchical symbol tree
search <pattern>      Structural search using tree-sitter S-expression queries
hover <file>:<line>:<col>  Type info, docs, and signature at a location
diagnostics [file]         Syntax errors and semantic diagnostics
rename <old> <new>         Rename a symbol (dry-run at syntactic precision)
dead [--kind K]            Find unreferenced symbols
callchain <symbol>         Multi-level call hierarchy (--depth N)
hierarchy <type>           Type hierarchy (supertypes and subtypes)
grammar list          Show installed and available languages
grammar install <L>   Install a language (or --all for everything)

## For agents: use --json
All commands support --json for structured output with metadata.
Prefer --json over default framed output for programmatic use.

## Output modes
Default: framed text with @@ file:line:column kind name @@ headers
--json: structured with resolution/completeness metadata (recommended for agents)
--raw: content only, no framing (pipe-friendly)

## Symbol resolution
Simple names: cq body handle_request
Qualified:    cq body Router::add_route (method on type)
              cq body api::routes::handle_request (module path)
Multiple matches returned as array; use --in <path> to narrow.

## Response examples

Framed (default):
@@ src/auth.rs:23:4 function authenticate @@
pub fn authenticate(req: &Request) -> Result<Auth> {
    let token = extract_token(req)?;
    verify(token)
}

JSON (--json):
{
  "resolution": "resolved",
  "completeness": "exhaustive",
  "definitions": [
    {"name": "authenticate", "kind": "function", "file": "src/auth.rs",
     "line": 23, "visibility": "pub"}
  ],
  "total": 1
}

## Precision metadata (JSON output)
resolution: "syntactic" | "resolved" | "semantic"
completeness: "exhaustive" | "best_effort"
- def/body/sig/outline/imports/context/tree/symbols/search: exhaustive
- refs/callers/deps: best_effort (may miss indirect refs)
- best_effort results include a "note" field explaining the limitation
- resolved > syntactic: follows imports and qualified names across files
- semantic > resolved: full type system (generics, traits, macros)

## Search command (S-expression queries)
Patterns use tree-sitter S-expression syntax directly.
Use `cq tree <file>` to explore node types for a language.
Example: cq search '(function_item name: (identifier) @name)'
Example: cq search '(function_item name: (identifier) @name (#eq? @name "main"))'
Captures (@name) control which part of the match is returned.

## Global flags
--project <path>  Explicit project root (default: auto-detect via .git, Cargo.toml, etc.)
--in <path>       Scope search to directory or file
--kind <K>        Filter by symbol kind (function, struct, class, trait, enum, etc.)
--lang <L>        Filter/force language
--limit <N>       Max results
--context <N>     Lines of context around matches (for refs, callers)
--depth <N>       Limit nesting depth (for tree, context)
--cache           Enable disk caching for repeated queries
--no-cache        Disable caching (use when files are changing)
--semantic        Use language server for type-resolved precision
--no-semantic     Disable LSP even if daemon is running

## Precision cascade (automatic, no configuration needed)
1. Daemon running?      --> semantic (sub-second, compiler-level)
2. --semantic flag?     --> oneshot LSP (start server, query, stop)
3. Stack graph rules?   --> resolved (follows imports, qualified names)
4. Fallback             --> syntactic (tree-sitter name matching)

## Daemon (optional, for fast semantic queries)
cq daemon start   Keep language servers warm
cq daemon stop    Shut down
cq daemon status  Show running servers
Auto-detected when running; no extra flags needed.

## Languages
71 languages supported. All auto-install on first use via WASM plugins.
cq grammar list -- see all available languages.
cq grammar install --all -- pre-download everything.

Scope-resolved (10): Rust, TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Go, C, C++, Java, Ruby, C#
LSP configs (40+): available for most languages when server is installed.
All 71: Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Rust, Go, C, C++, Java, Ruby, PHP,
  C#, Swift, Kotlin, HTML, CSS, JSON, YAML, TOML, Elixir, Haskell, Dart,
  SQL, Scala, Zig, Lua, Bash, Dockerfile, Terraform, Markdown, XML,
  Protobuf, GraphQL, Vue, Svelte, F#, Groovy, Objective-C, Nix, CMake,
  SCSS, Elm, Solidity, Verilog, CUDA, Fortran, Ada, Pascal, LaTeX,
  Prisma, Bicep, and more.

## Exit codes
0 = results found
1 = no results (valid query, nothing matched)
2 = usage error (bad args)
3 = project error (no root found)
4 = parse error (reported in warnings, does not block other results)

## Efficient agent usage
- Use --json for all programmatic access. Parse resolution/completeness to calibrate trust.
- cq body > reading files. One function, zero noise, minimal tokens.
- cq context file:line for navigating from errors/stack traces to functions.
- cq def + cq refs for impact analysis before changes.
- cq outline to orient in unfamiliar files; cq tree for directory-level orientation.
- cq sig for API contracts without reading implementations.
- cq deps to understand a function's dependency footprint before refactoring.
- Use qualified names to disambiguate: cq body Router::add_route.
- Use --in to scope searches and avoid noise in large codebases.
- Pipe --raw output directly into other tools (wc, diff, etc.).
- Any language works -- if not installed, cq downloads the grammar automatically.
